name: Mongolia

colors.symbolism: prosperity, progress, the joy of the people
the eternal blue sky (Tengri) of the Mongolian steppe — the supreme deity of traditional Mongolian shamanism
the colour of the Soyombo emblem; in Tibetan Buddhist tradition, the Yellow Hat (Gelug) sect to which most Mongolian Buddhists belong

description: Three equal vertical stripes — red, blue, red. Centred on the inner edge of the hoist red stripe is the Soyombo: a vertical yellow emblem comprising a flame, sun and crescent moon, two triangles (top and bottom), two horizontal rectangles bracketing a yin-yang, and two vertical rectangles flanking the whole composition.
history: Mongolia's flag in this overall design (red-blue-red vertical tricolour with the yellow Soyombo) was adopted on 12 February 1992 with the new democratic constitution. The previous communist-era flag had a five-pointed star above the Soyombo, which was removed in 1992. The red-blue-red layout itself was first used in 1949. The Soyombo emblem was created by the lama-prince Zanabazar in the 17th century, originally as a script (the Soyombo script); the column-of-symbols form on the flag has been in use since 1911.

features.symbolism: red for prosperity and progress (and the joys of the Mongolian people); blue for the eternal blue sky (Tengri) of the Mongolian steppe — the country's most fundamental natural and spiritual symbol

the Soyombo: a vertical column of stylised symbols read top to bottom — flame (representing past, present and future prosperity), sun and crescent moon (the eternity of the Mongol nation), two triangles (arrowheads pointing at enemies of the people), two horizontal rectangles (honesty and uprightness), the taijitu yin-yang (the harmony of male and female), and two vertical rectangles (the strength of friendship). Designed by Zanabazar in the 17th century, the Soyombo has been used as a Mongolian national symbol since.
